Row.Text: Turn-by-turn graphs, civilization rankings, global relations, war history, and Triumph progress for every met civilization.
Track each civilization across the course of the game.
Composite power score, the same number the Victory screen uses.
Total number of cities and towns owned.
Number of fully-researched technology nodes in the current age tree.
Number of fully-unlocked civic nodes in the current age tree.
Estimated economic output based on per-turn yields and turns elapsed.
Science per turn, drives technology research.
Culture per turn, drives civics progress.
Net food output per turn across all settlements.
Summary of current diplomatic standing across met civilizations and suzerain city-states.
Scaled real-world population, exponentially derived from total citizen count.
Net diplomatic Influence generated per turn.
Active diplomatic actions, endeavors, sanctions, and espionage involving this leader.
Number of active trade routes leaving this player's cities.
Total territory owned in square kilometers, scaled from controlled hex tiles.
No crisis has begun in this age yet.
No wars yet. Once any civ declares war, you can graph it here.
No data recorded for this war.
Percentage of the settlement cap this player has used. 100% means at the limit.
How close the current age is to ending. Same for all civilizations.
Current crisis stage (0 = none, 3 = peak crisis). Game-wide value.
Summed combat strength across all of this player's military units (land, naval, and air formations).
Total wonders constructed by this player across all ages.
This civilization is still unmet. Enable "Show names of unmet civilizations and city-states" in Options to show real names.
This city-state is still unmet. Enable "Show names of unmet civilizations and city-states" in Options to show real names.
Show names of unmet civilizations and city-states
When off, unmet civilizations and city-states appear as generic placeholders in charts, the rankings, and global relations.
No civilization has made progress yet.
No samples yet, play a turn and reopen.
Hide information and stats for civilizations you haven't met yet.
Start stats tracking upon meeting a new civilization
Overall crisis impact across all ages
Chart failed to render, see UI.log.
No resource samples yet, once you assign your first resource the chart will populate.
Current age: {1_Age}. Cells show triggered / total available; bar is filled percent.

The number of your units killed in combat over this war (a body count: one per unit destroyed), companion to the Military Power figure above. The tooltip also shows a scaled ≈ soldiers-killed estimate (each unit ≈ a formation that grows with the era), comparable to the scaled population metric.
Population displaced by siege, pillage, or conquest during the war and resettled elsewhere, contributed by the Emigration mod when it is installed.

Your standing Military Power (the total combat strength of your army) at the end of the war (or the latest turn while it is ongoing). This is the size of your forces, not a loss; "Military Power Lost" below tracks the casualties.

Combat strength of your units actually killed in combat, summed as each unit dies (its base combat strength). This is true casualties, building replacements never hides a loss, and the figure is not derived from the size of your standing army. Wars fought before this tracking existed instead fall back to the net drop in your army's total strength.

Net settlements won or lost by capture: a settlement you capture counts +1, one captured from you counts −1. Founding a new settlement with a settler does not count, only settlements that actually change hands. Because it is a net figure, a settlement you lose and then retake cancels back to zero. It is recorded from capture events, so a war fought before this was added shows no figure.

How many of your settlements were permanently destroyed (razed). It is charged to you as the owner from before the city was captured, not to the conqueror who razed it. Unlike a capture, which can be reversed by retaking the city, a razed city is gone for good. Only recorded for wars fought after this tracking was added.

Territory that actually changed hands in the war, in square kilometres (each map tile is about 7,000 km²). Counts only the land of settlements captured from or lost to the enemy; peaceful expansion and border growth are excluded. A positive number means you took ground; a negative number means you lost it.

Total population you lost, added up from every turn your population fell, turns where it grew are ignored, so normal growth can never mask wartime losses. Two limits: it cannot tell a war death from a famine in the same span, and population from a city you later recapture still counts as lost on the turn it first fell.

Food disruption: every turn your crop yield (net food per turn) fell, that drop is added up (turns it rose are ignored, so ordinary growth can't hide it). Big drops come from pillaged farms and tiles and from cities being occupied or lost. It is a sum of rate drops, a proxy for disruption, not a total amount of food.

Production disruption: every turn your production-per-turn fell, that drop is added up (turns it rose are ignored, so ordinary economic growth can't hide it). Big drops come from pillaged tiles and cities being occupied or lost. It is a sum of rate drops, a proxy for disruption, not a total amount of production. Production you deliberately poured into the war is tracked separately under "Directed to War".

Production you spent on the war effort: the production cost of every military unit and military or fortification building (walls, etc.) you completed while the war was on, added up. Civilian units, economic buildings, and wonders don't count. This is deliberate investment in the war, not a loss, so it is shown as a neutral figure. The cost is the effective cost in the city that built it, your leader, civilization, and policy discounts are already baked in. Items bought outright with gold or faith aren't counted, since that's treasury, not production; production overflow between items isn't split out exactly.
No wars yet. Once any civ declares war, the timeline will populate.
No wars match the current filters.
No samples yet, play a turn first.
CSV is too large ({1_SizeMB} MB). Lower the history cap in Options and try again.
Copied · {1_Rows} rows × {2_Cols} cols{3_SizeTag} · paste into Excel / Sheets / a .csv file
Clipboard export unavailable. CSV written to UI.log.
History resets at each age transition.
As there is no method of Data persistence,
a single chart cannot span multiple ages
Use current age or an age range filter.

An estimated economic output score based on per-turn yields and turns elapsed.

It is a display metric for comparing civilizations over time. It does not affect game state.

A summary of current diplomatic relationships with met civilizations and suzerain city-states.

Higher values reflect stronger relations. Lower values reflect hostility or war. This is a display metric only.
No snapshot yet, the age hasn't ended.
Show all civs at full opacity
Toggle X-axis time units between turn number, in-game year, or both
Hide wonder-built markers on chart lines
Show wonder-built markers on chart lines (icon at the turn each civ completed a wonder)
Copies all sampled turns for all civilizations to the clipboard as CSV. Paste into Excel, Sheets, or another spreadsheet tool to save or analyze the data.
Copy all sampled history as CSV
Click a metric column to sort the civilizations by it. Use the Rank / Value toggle to switch what each cell shows.
War Impact, each combatant's trajectory through a chosen war
Show real names for civilizations I haven't met (spoiler)
Colorblind mode (high-contrast palette for charts and relations)
Show eliminated civs in charts and rankings
Show war onset markers on the Emigration refugees chart
Show disaster onset markers on the Emigration refugees chart
Show wonder markers on chart lines
Unlimited (very large saves, may slow performance)
Current effective cap: {1_Cap} · Game speed detected: {2_Speed} · Hard ceiling: {3_Ceiling}

Higher values take a snapshot less often, so the mod does less work each turn, lighter on slow machines and long games. Applies next turn; existing samples are kept. If the Emigration mod is also installed, lower this first for combined-mode relief, then raise Emigration's turn interval.
Disable downsampling and keep every recorded turn after the cap is reached. Best used with a high cap or Unlimited.
Clear all recorded wars? Sample history and other data will be kept.
Ring node = met major civ. Multiple lines between two civs = multiple relationships.
Viewer-civ ↔ city-state relationships. Multiple lines = multiple relationships.
The Coming of the Sea Peoples
The Coming of the Horse Lords
The Crisis of the Third Century
History sample cap {1_Cap}, older samples are downsampled to fit.
History sample cap {1_Cap}, no downsampling yet.
Reveal full history when you meet a civilization
On: meeting a civ reveals its entire past on the charts. Off: lines begin at the moment of first contact.
Demographics paused due to engine errors.
Your towns, read live, specialization, size, and growth at a glance.
You have no towns. Found or convert a settlement to see it here.
Heuristic suggestions from each town's tiles, size, and yields. Advice only, nothing changes in-game.
Strong gold output favors a trade focus.
Large population ({1_Count}) suits an urban center.
Exposed frontier ({1_Count} open borders) suits a fort.
Best overall match for this town.
No standout tiles yet, keep growing (food).
Pop {1_Pop} is below {2_Min}; grow before converting.
Pop {1_Pop} and affordable, a city unlocks production and districts.
Chosen to balance your towns' roles.
Town-heavy empire: {1_Towns} towns vs {2_Cities} cities. A new city adds production and districts.
Population is climbing; urbanizing compounds the growth.
Population has stalled; a growth focus may help more than specializing.
Unavailable: you haven't met this civilization yet.
Unavailable: you haven't discovered this settlement yet.
Recognized as the {1_Ord} greatest settlement in the world.
A natural wonder graces its lands.

"Score" here is a civilization's total output: its settlements' combined food, production, gold, science, and culture. It is not the official game Score in the Victories screen, which is calculated differently.
As host, this limit applies to comparative analytics for ALL players this game.
The host sets the maximum analytics visibility this game; you can restrict your own view further.
Host analytics policy: {1}. Comparative data is limited for all players.
Core stat pages only; advanced graphs, the relations network, and tuning controls are hidden.
All graphs and tabs; advanced storage and sampling controls stay hidden.
Everything, including storage cap, decimation, and sample-rate controls.
Age triumph progress: the legacy-path radar for each civilization.
Resource allocation by category over time.
War timeline (Gantt) and per-war detail graphs.
Crisis stages and their per-civilization costs.
Number of Trade Routes over Time
